one_line_takeaway: PIDP-Attack shows that pairing a query-time prompt-injection suffix with a small set of database-poisoned passages removes the query-foreknowledge requirement of prior RAG poisoning attacks (PoisonedRAG), reaching ~98% average attack success and beating single-vector baselines (which fall to near 0% ASR) across three QA datasets and eight LLMs.
key_finding: PIDP-Attack achieves an average ASR of 98.125% across the main comparison table, versus 1.875% for GCG (prompt-injection-only) and 3.125% for Corpus (poisoning-only), and versus 92% for PoisonedRAG and 97.167% for PR-Attack cited as prior best (Table 1). In the head-to-head main results (Table 4, n=5, k=5, strict matching), PIDP-Attack improves ASR over PoisonedRAG by +4% to +16% on Natural Questions across 7 of 8 models (e.g. +0.16 on gpt-oss-20b, +0.12 on gpt-oss-120b), by +5% to +12% on MS-MARCO, and matches or slightly exceeds PoisonedRAG on the near-saturated HotpotQA benchmark (near 100% on 7 of 8 models). Ablations show the compound mechanism is necessary: prompt-only injection (A1) yields 0% relaxed success on NQ and MS-MARCO across all three tested models; enabling retrieval on the injected query without poisoning (A2, Clean-RAG) raises relaxed success to 68%-96% on some models but remains unreliable and dataset-dependent (e.g. 0% on HotpotQA for qwen2.5-7b, 15% on MS-MARCO for llama-3.1-8b). Budget sweeps (A3) show PIDP-Attack reaches >95% ASR with as few as n=2 poisoned passages on NQ and HotpotQA, but MS-MARCO climbs more gradually from ~30% at n=1 to >90% at n=5, correlating with lower retrieval F1 on that corpus (0.836 for PIDP on MS-MARCO vs. 0.992 on NQ and 1.000 on HotpotQA, Table 5). Context sweeps (A4) reveal a non-monotonic dilution effect: e.g. qwen2.5-7b's ASR on MS-MARCO drops from 97% at k=5 to 82% at k=10 as additional clean passages dilute the poisoned evidence.
core_contribution: The paper proposes PIDP-Attack (Prompt Injection + Database Poisoning Attack), a compound black-box attack on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems that combines (1) a lightweight query-path prompt-injection suffix delta(S), appended to an arbitrary victim query q at inference time (via a compromised client plugin, reverse proxy, or middleware), with (2) corpus-path poisoning: injecting a small, fixed set of n adversarial passages (default n=5) into the retrieval database, each constructed as p_i = S + '.' + b_i, where S is an attacker-chosen target question and b_i is an LLM-generated (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) supporting body for an incorrect target answer a-minus. The key novelty over prior poisoning attacks (e.g., PoisonedRAG) is that the attacker does not need advance knowledge of the victim's actual query q -- the injected suffix steers the retriever toward the pre-positioned poisoned passages regardless of what q is, coercing the generator to answer the attacker's chosen target question S instead. The authors formalize this as a joint optimization over the injection suffix delta and poison set P to maximize the probability that the target incorrect answer a-minus appears in the model output across a distribution of victim queries.
threat_model: Black-box RAG pipeline with three components: retrieval corpus D, retriever R (embedding-based, e.g. Contriever with dot-product scoring), and generator G (an LLM), treated as black boxes with no access to internal parameters. Two orthogonal attacker capabilities are assumed: (1) query-path manipulation -- the attacker can append a suffix to the user-visible query at inference time without user cooperation or knowledge of the query's content; (2) corpus-path manipulation -- the attacker can insert a small number of adversarial passages into the retrieval corpus via an automated ingestion channel, open contribution mechanism, or compromised ETL pipeline, with n small relative to |D|. The attacker cannot modify retriever/LLM parameters, disable safety filters, or change the system prompt -- the attack is purely input/data-plane manipulation, not training-time. The attack is stated to be most applicable when the query string is treated as fully trusted (no robust quoting/segmentation), corpus updates lack strong provenance checks, and the generator tends to follow high-priority instructions in the query/context. Success is defined as the exact target answer string a-minus appearing in the model's response (strict matching), with a relaxed keyword-based diagnostic used for two ablation-only conditions.
limitations: The paper explicitly reports that PIDP-Attack sometimes underperforms PoisonedRAG on models not shown in the main table, attributing this to weaker instruction-following (smaller/refusal-centric models) and noisier retrieval that misaligns the injected query with poisoned evidence. Three failure modes are documented: retrieval-limited (poisoned passages fail to enter top-k, e.g. MS-MARCO at n=1 where retrieval F1 drops below 30% and ASR falls under 50%), generation-limited (models with strong priors or rigid instruction-following ignore injected context even when poison is retrieved), and dilution-limited (larger k reduces the relative influence of any single poisoned passage, producing non-monotonic ASR). Some safety-aligned/guard-style models refuse across all attacks (ASR near 0), meaning effectiveness is model-dependent rather than universal. The evaluation deliberately omits guardrails such as instruction/context separation or query sanitization from its RAG prompt wrapper to measure 'unmitigated risk,' so results characterize a worst-case baseline rather than hardened deployments. The paper does not release ready-to-deploy poison indices for commercial platforms, and full implementation details of the injection-template optimization are deferred to an appendix not captured in the reviewed page range.

datasets_used: MS-MARCO, BEIR train split used strictly as evaluation split
quantitative_results: Average ASR across baselines (Table 1): Corpus 1.875%, GCG 3.125%, Clean-RAG (ablation) 45.778%, GGPP 82.875%, Disinformation Attack 88.333%, PoisonedRAG 92%, PR-Attack 97.167%, PIDP-Attack 98.125%

PIDP-Attack vs PoisonedRAG on NQ: +4% to +16% ASR improvement across 7 of 8 models (Table 4); e.g. gpt-oss-20b PoisonedRAG 0.80+/-0.12 vs PIDP 0.96+/-0.05 (+0.16); gpt-oss-120b 0.88+/-0.10 vs 1.00+/-0.00 (+0.12)
PIDP-Attack vs PoisonedRAG on MS-MARCO: +5% to +12% improvement (Table 4); e.g. gpt-oss-120b 0.77+/-0.11 vs 0.89+/-0.05 (+0.12)
HotpotQA: PIDP-Attack matches or exceeds PoisonedRAG, near 100% ASR on 7 of 8 models (Table 4)
GCG (prompt-only) and Corpus (poison-only) ASR under 1% on NQ and MS-MARCO under strict matching (Table 4)

Retrieval F1 for adversarial passages (Table 5): PIDP 0.992 (NQ), 1.000 (HotpotQA), 0.836 (MS-MARCO); PoisonedRAG 0.962 (NQ), 0.998 (HotpotQA), 0.884 (MS-MARCO); GGPP 0.826 (NQ), 0.998 (HotpotQA), 0.598 (MS-MARCO); Corpus near 0 on all three (0.024, 0.022, 0.000)

Ablation A1/A2 (Table 6, relaxed matching): Prompt-only 0.00+/-0.00 on NQ and MS-MARCO for all three tested models; Clean-RAG ranges from 0.00 (qwen2.5-7b on NQ and HotpotQA) up to 0.98+/-0.06 (llama-3.1-8b on HotpotQA) and 0.96+/-0.05 (qwen2-7b on MS-MARCO)
Poison budget sweep (A3): PIDP-Attack reaches >95% ASR at n=2 on NQ and HotpotQA for Llama-3 and Qwen models; on MS-MARCO ASR climbs from ~30% at n=1 to >90% at n=5
Context budget sweep (A4): qwen2.5-7b ASR on MS-MARCO drops from 97% at k=5 to 82% at k=10
baselines_compared: PR-Attack (cited comparison in Table 1, not run as a live baseline in Table 4)
